ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Glasgow Coma Scale and Level of Consciousness
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Glasgow Coma Scale and Level of Consciousness | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Glasgow Coma Scale and Level of Consciousness — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Glasgow Coma Scale and Level of Consciousness** is core NCLEX-RN neurological nursing: you translate **assessment** into **risk**, protect **airway and aspiration**, watch for **sudden focal deficits** or **altered LOC**, and **communicate** with objective data. Boards hide urgency behind “stable” wording—compare **new change** to baseline when the stem allows.

Canadian items may use **metric** vitals and interprofessional notes; **first action** logic matches US stems: **ABC**, **neuro checks**, **seizure safety**, **ICP-friendly positioning** per orders, then **notify** when thresholds are met.

Cross-link [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Assessment**: LOC, pupils, motor strength by side, speech, facial symmetry, sensation when ordered.
- **Safety**: seizure precautions, bed low, pads, **nothing per mouth** if dysphagia suspected until screened.
- **Escalation**: new deficit, sustained **GCS** drop, **repeated seizures**, **post-tPA** neuro change, **ICP** red flags.
- **Teaching** (when stable): medication timing, injury prevention, when to call EMS.
- **Documentation**: objective trends and times for neuro events.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client with an acute neurological concern related to **Glasgow Coma Scale and Level of Consciousness** develops **worsening confusion** and **unequal grips** during your shift. **Vitals** show **rising blood pressure** and **irregular respiratory effort**.

**Fork:** Your priority is **focused neuro reassessment**, **airway and oxygenation support** per orders, **seizure precautions** if indicated, and **notify the provider** with **SBAR** including **time of symptom change**—not routine hygiene first.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

**Related:** [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window) · [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning) · [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

preTest.question: For Glasgow Coma Scale and Level of Consciousness, which action best matches NCLEX-RN prioritization?
options: Complete discharge paperwork before reporting a sudden drop in GCS
Reassess airway, oxygenation, and neuro status; notify the provider when acute change criteria are met
Send the nursing assistant alone to evaluate new-onset unilateral weakness
Delay assessment to finish a scheduled non-urgent phone call
preTest.rationale: Neurological deterioration requires immediate reassessment, airway and oxygenation protection, and timely provider communication before routine or deferrable tasks.

preTest.question: A client with risk related to Glasgow Coma Scale and Level of Consciousness reports new numbness and slurred speech. What is the priority?
options: Offer juice and crackers for comfort
Activate stroke precautions, obtain vitals and glucose if ordered, and notify the provider with objective findings
Ambulate the client to rule out anxiety
Wait for the next scheduled neuro check in four hours
preTest.rationale: New focal neuro deficits with speech change are treated as time-sensitive until evaluated; nursing prioritizes assessment, safety, and urgent communication.

postTest.question: Which finding most urgently escalates care in a neuro context for Glasgow Coma Scale and Level of Consciousness?
options: Mild dry lips in afebrile client
Sudden asymmetric pupils with declining LOC after head injury
Request for a second pillow
Stable chronic tremor unchanged from baseline
postTest.rationale: Anisocoria with declining consciousness suggests herniation or expanding mass until proven otherwise and requires immediate escalation and monitoring.

postTest.question: When is patient teaching about Glasgow Coma Scale and Level of Consciousness most appropriate?
options: During active seizure or unprotected airway risk
After stabilization when the client is alert, oriented, and able to participate
Only at home without nurse follow-up
Before any assessment to save time
postTest.rationale: Education is effective and safe after life threats are stabilized and the patient can engage—not during active seizure or airway compromise.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Stroke: Ischemic vs Hemorrhagic
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Stroke: Ischemic vs Hemorrhagic | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Stroke: Ischemic vs Hemorrhagic — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Stroke: Ischemic vs Hemorrhagic** focuses on **acute neurovascular** care: time-sensitive **reperfusion** eligibility, **bleeding risk** with thrombolytics, **aspiration** prevention, and **neuro checks** after intervention. NCLEX-RN rewards knowing **what delays harm** (silent aspiration, missed deficit, hypotension) versus **routine tasks**.

Differentiate **ischemic** versus **hemorrhagic** patterns when the stem gives **CT** or **presentation** clues—**tPA/TNK** themes apply only when **contraindications** are absent per item framing.

Integrate [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [stroke & ICP nursing gold](LESSON:stroke-increased-icp-gold), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Watch for **tPA exclusion** traps (recent surgery, uncontrolled HTN, improving symptoms) and **hemorrhagic conversion** clues.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **FAST**-style recognition themes; **last known well** documentation.
- **NPO** and **aspiration** precautions when swallow at risk.
- **Thrombolytic**: strict inclusion/exclusion per protocol—**bleeding checks** after.
- **Post-stroke**: DVT prophylaxis per orders, **mobility** when safe, **mood** screening themes.
- **Avoid** giving aspirin before **hemorrhagic** stroke ruled out when stem implies bleed.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 71-year-old arrives with **sudden left arm weakness** and **slurred speech**; **last known well** 70 minutes ago. **BP** is **188/96**, **glucose** **6.2 mmol/L** (Canadian stem), **CT** reportedly shows **no bleed**.

**Fork:** **Activate stroke pathway** themes—**continuous monitoring**, **NPO**, **prepare** for **thrombolytic** eligibility screening per protocol, **avoid** **hypotension** from unnecessary antihypertensives unless ordered for extreme BP—communicate **neuro changes** immediately after intervention.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

**Related:** [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window) · [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning) · [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Post-Stroke Complications and Aspiration Prevention
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Post-Stroke Complications and Aspiration Prevention | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Post-Stroke Complications and Aspiration Prevention — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Post-Stroke Complications and Aspiration Prevention** focuses on **acute neurovascular** care: time-sensitive **reperfusion** eligibility, **bleeding risk** with thrombolytics, **aspiration** prevention, and **neuro checks** after intervention. NCLEX-RN rewards knowing **what delays harm** (silent aspiration, missed deficit, hypotension) versus **routine tasks**.

Differentiate **ischemic** versus **hemorrhagic** patterns when the stem gives **CT** or **presentation** clues—**tPA/TNK** themes apply only when **contraindications** are absent per item framing.

Integrate [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [stroke & ICP nursing gold](LESSON:stroke-increased-icp-gold), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Watch for **tPA exclusion** traps (recent surgery, uncontrolled HTN, improving symptoms) and **hemorrhagic conversion** clues.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **FAST**-style recognition themes; **last known well** documentation.
- **NPO** and **aspiration** precautions when swallow at risk.
- **Thrombolytic**: strict inclusion/exclusion per protocol—**bleeding checks** after.
- **Post-stroke**: DVT prophylaxis per orders, **mobility** when safe, **mood** screening themes.
- **Avoid** giving aspirin before **hemorrhagic** stroke ruled out when stem implies bleed.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 71-year-old arrives with **sudden left arm weakness** and **slurred speech**; **last known well** 70 minutes ago. **BP** is **188/96**, **glucose** **6.2 mmol/L** (Canadian stem), **CT** reportedly shows **no bleed**.

**Fork:** **Activate stroke pathway** themes—**continuous monitoring**, **NPO**, **prepare** for **thrombolytic** eligibility screening per protocol, **avoid** **hypotension** from unnecessary antihypertensives unless ordered for extreme BP—communicate **neuro changes** immediately after intervention.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Seizure Types and Nursing Priorities
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Seizure Types and Nursing Priorities | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Seizure Types and Nursing Priorities — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Seizure Types and Nursing Priorities** covers **seizure recognition**, **airway protection**, **rescue medications** per orders, and **continuous monitoring** for **recurrence** or **status epilepticus**. Nurses prevent **injury**, time events, and prepare for **labs** and **EEG** themes when instability persists.

Medication items probe **therapeutic monitoring** (levels, sedation, rash) and **patient teaching** for adherence and driving safety when stable.

Use [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Status epilepticus stems push **timed benzodiazepine** pathways and **airway** first.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Types**: focal aware/impaired, generalized tonic-clonic, absence themes—match **safety** to type.
- **Status**: treat as **emergency**; protect airway; **timed** rescue meds per ACLS/policy.
- **Meds**: titrate per orders; watch **SJS** rash signals, **hyponatremia** with some agents when stem cues.
- **EEG**: reduce stimuli; safety with **ambulatory** EEG themes when shown.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client has **back-to-back tonic-clonic seizures** without full recovery between. **SpO₂** drifts to **88%** with **secretions**.

**Fork:** **Protect airway**, **call for help**, **give rescue** therapy per order/protocol, **prepare** for **intubation** themes if ventilation fails—**not** leaving to complete another client’s discharge teaching.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Traumatic Brain Injury
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Traumatic Brain Injury | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Traumatic Brain Injury — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Traumatic Brain Injury** ties **intracranial pressure dynamics** to **assessment** and **interventions**: **head-of-bed** per order, **avoid harmful neck flexion**, **sedation/analgesia** themes, **osmotherapy** per protocol, and **early recognition** of **herniation** patterns. NCLEX punishes **rough suctioning** or **clustered noxious stimuli** without a plan when ICP risk is high.

Pair [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [stroke & ICP nursing gold](LESSON:stroke-increased-icp-gold), [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Items contrast **early subtle** changes with **late** **Cushing** patterns—choose escalation when late signs appear.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Early**: headache, nausea, subtle pupil change, BP trends.
- **Late**: **Cushing** triad patterns, posturing, rapid **GCS** drop.
- **Positioning**: **head midline**, **HOB** per order—avoid **extreme** hip flexion if contraindicated.
- **Shunt**: **reduced** ICP signs with infection can still be **obstruction**—report **worsening** headache or vomiting.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Post **TBI**, the client’s **GCS** drops from **14 to 10**, **pupil** becomes **sluggish** on one side, and **BP** rises.

**Fork:** **ICP crisis** until cleared—**notify neurosurgery/provider**, **elevate HOB** per order, **avoid** **jugular** compression, **prepare** for **mannitol/hypertonic saline** per orders, **frequent neuro checks**.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

**Related:** [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window) · [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning) · [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

preTest.question: For Traumatic Brain Injury, which action best matches NCLEX-RN prioritization?
options: Complete discharge paperwork before reporting a sudden drop in GCS
Reassess airway, oxygenation, and neuro status; notify the provider when acute change criteria are met
Send the nursing assistant alone to evaluate new-onset unilateral weakness
Delay assessment to finish a scheduled non-urgent phone call
preTest.rationale: Neurological deterioration requires immediate reassessment, airway and oxygenation protection, and timely provider communication before routine or deferrable tasks.

preTest.question: A client with risk related to Traumatic Brain Injury reports new numbness and slurred speech. What is the priority?
options: Offer juice and crackers for comfort
Activate stroke precautions, obtain vitals and glucose if ordered, and notify the provider with objective findings
Ambulate the client to rule out anxiety
Wait for the next scheduled neuro check in four hours
preTest.rationale: New focal neuro deficits with speech change are treated as time-sensitive until evaluated; nursing prioritizes assessment, safety, and urgent communication.

postTest.question: Which finding most urgently escalates care in a neuro context for Traumatic Brain Injury?
options: Mild dry lips in afebrile client
Sudden asymmetric pupils with declining LOC after head injury
Request for a second pillow
Stable chronic tremor unchanged from baseline
postTest.rationale: Anisocoria with declining consciousness suggests herniation or expanding mass until proven otherwise and requires immediate escalation and monitoring.

postTest.question: When is patient teaching about Traumatic Brain Injury most appropriate?
options: During active seizure or unprotected airway risk
After stabilization when the client is alert, oriented, and able to participate
Only at home without nurse follow-up
Before any assessment to save time
postTest.rationale: Education is effective and safe after life threats are stabilized and the patient can engage—not during active seizure or airway compromise.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Concussion Nursing Care
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Concussion Nursing Care | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Concussion Nursing Care — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Concussion Nursing Care** is core NCLEX-RN neurological nursing: you translate **assessment** into **risk**, protect **airway and aspiration**, watch for **sudden focal deficits** or **altered LOC**, and **communicate** with objective data. Boards hide urgency behind “stable” wording—compare **new change** to baseline when the stem allows.

Canadian items may use **metric** vitals and interprofessional notes; **first action** logic matches US stems: **ABC**, **neuro checks**, **seizure safety**, **ICP-friendly positioning** per orders, then **notify** when thresholds are met.

Cross-link [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Assessment**: LOC, pupils, motor strength by side, speech, facial symmetry, sensation when ordered.
- **Safety**: seizure precautions, bed low, pads, **nothing per mouth** if dysphagia suspected until screened.
- **Escalation**: new deficit, sustained **GCS** drop, **repeated seizures**, **post-tPA** neuro change, **ICP** red flags.
- **Teaching** (when stable): medication timing, injury prevention, when to call EMS.
- **Documentation**: objective trends and times for neuro events.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client with an acute neurological concern related to **Concussion Nursing Care** develops **worsening confusion** and **unequal grips** during your shift. **Vitals** show **rising blood pressure** and **irregular respiratory effort**.

**Fork:** Your priority is **focused neuro reassessment**, **airway and oxygenation support** per orders, **seizure precautions** if indicated, and **notify the provider** with **SBAR** including **time of symptom change**—not routine hygiene first.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Skull Fractures and CSF Leak — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Skull Fractures and CSF Leak** ties **intracranial pressure dynamics** to **assessment** and **interventions**: **head-of-bed** per order, **avoid harmful neck flexion**, **sedation/analgesia** themes, **osmotherapy** per protocol, and **early recognition** of **herniation** patterns. NCLEX punishes **rough suctioning** or **clustered noxious stimuli** without a plan when ICP risk is high.

Pair [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [stroke & ICP nursing gold](LESSON:stroke-increased-icp-gold), [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance

Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Items contrast **early subtle** changes with **late** **Cushing** patterns—choose escalation when late signs appear.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Early**: headache, nausea, subtle pupil change, BP trends.
- **Late**: **Cushing** triad patterns, posturing, rapid **GCS** drop.
- **Positioning**: **head midline**, **HOB** per order—avoid **extreme** hip flexion if contraindicated.
- **Shunt**: **reduced** ICP signs with infection can still be **obstruction**—report **worsening** headache or vomiting.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Post **TBI**, the client’s **GCS** drops from **14 to 10**, **pupil** becomes **sluggish** on one side, and **BP** rises.

**Fork:** **ICP crisis** until cleared—**notify neurosurgery/provider**, **elevate HOB** per order, **avoid** **jugular** compression, **prepare** for **mannitol/hypertonic saline** per orders, **frequent neuro checks**.

- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Spinal Cord Injury: Levels and Motor/Sensory Loss
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Spinal Cord Injury: Levels and Motor/Sensory Loss | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Spinal Cord Injury: Levels and Motor/Sensory Loss — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Spinal Cord Injury: Levels and Motor/Sensory Loss** applies to **spinal cord injury** clients: **level** determines expected **motor/sensory** loss, **neurogenic shock** (hypotension + relative bradycardia themes), and **autonomic dysreflexia** above the lesion. Nursing priorities include **hemodynamic monitoring**, **bladder/bowel** programs, **skin protection**, and **rapid** response to **sudden hypertension** with **headache**.

Link [SCI autonomic dysreflexia](LESSON:spinal-cord-injury-autonomic-dysreflexia), [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. **AD** questions reward **sitting up**, **removing noxious stimulus**, and **antihypertensive** per order—not ignoring sudden **BP spike** in T6+ injuries.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Levels**: Cervical vs thoracic loss patterns on exams—know **phrenic** risk with high cervical themes.
- **Neurogenic shock**: fluids/vasopressors per orders—different from **spinal shock** wording in some items.
- **AD**: trigger removal, **sit up**, **loosen** constrictive clothing, **notify**—not “finish dressing change first.”
- **Skin and DVT** prevention across rehab phases.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A **T4 SCI** client on the ward reports **pounding headache** and **flushed skin** 20 minutes after **Foley occlusion** is suspected.

**Fork:** **Autonomic dysreflexia**—**sit the patient up**, **loosen clothing**, **check catheter** and **rectum** for noxious stimulus per training, **monitor BP**, **notify provider**—**not** “reassure and continue rounds.”

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

**Related:** [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window) · [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning) · [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Neurogenic Shock
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Neurogenic Shock | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Neurogenic Shock — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Neurogenic Shock** applies to **spinal cord injury** clients: **level** determines expected **motor/sensory** loss, **neurogenic shock** (hypotension + relative bradycardia themes), and **autonomic dysreflexia** above the lesion. Nursing priorities include **hemodynamic monitoring**, **bladder/bowel** programs, **skin protection**, and **rapid** response to **sudden hypertension** with **headache**.

Link [SCI autonomic dysreflexia](LESSON:spinal-cord-injury-autonomic-dysreflexia), [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. **AD** questions reward **sitting up**, **removing noxious stimulus**, and **antihypertensive** per order—not ignoring sudden **BP spike** in T6+ injuries.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Levels**: Cervical vs thoracic loss patterns on exams—know **phrenic** risk with high cervical themes.
- **Neurogenic shock**: fluids/vasopressors per orders—different from **spinal shock** wording in some items.
- **AD**: trigger removal, **sit up**, **loosen** constrictive clothing, **notify**—not “finish dressing change first.”
- **Skin and DVT** prevention across rehab phases.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:

- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Alzheimer Disease and Dementia Safety
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Alzheimer Disease and Dementia Safety | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Alzheimer Disease and Dementia Safety — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Alzheimer Disease and Dementia Safety** is core NCLEX-RN neurological nursing: you translate **assessment** into **risk**, protect **airway and aspiration**, watch for **sudden focal deficits** or **altered LOC**, and **communicate** with objective data. Boards hide urgency behind “stable” wording—compare **new change** to baseline when the stem allows.

Canadian items may use **metric** vitals and interprofessional notes; **first action** logic matches US stems: **ABC**, **neuro checks**, **seizure safety**, **ICP-friendly positioning** per orders, then **notify** when thresholds are met.

Cross-link [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Assessment**: LOC, pupils, motor strength by side, speech, facial symmetry, sensation when ordered.
- **Safety**: seizure precautions, bed low, pads, **nothing per mouth** if dysphagia suspected until screened.
- **Escalation**: new deficit, sustained **GCS** drop, **repeated seizures**, **post-tPA** neuro change, **ICP** red flags.
- **Teaching** (when stable): medication timing, injury prevention, when to call EMS.
- **Documentation**: objective trends and times for neuro events.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client with an acute neurological concern related to **Alzheimer Disease and Dementia Safety** develops **worsening confusion** and **unequal grips** during your shift. **Vitals** show **rising blood pressure** and **irregular respiratory effort**.

**Fork:** Your priority is **focused neuro reassessment**, **airway and oxygenation support** per orders, **seizure precautions** if indicated, and **notify the provider** with **SBAR** including **time of symptom change**—not routine hygiene first.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Delirium vs Dementia vs Depression | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Delirium vs Dementia vs Depression — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Delirium vs Dementia vs Depression** trains **NGN-style prioritization**: pick the client with **airway compromise**, **new unilateral weakness**, **seizing activity**, **sudden LOC drop**, **Cushing triad**, **autonomic dysreflexia**, or **post-tPA bleeding** over stable teaching or routine meds. Boards reward **objective instability** over polite requests.

Anchor with [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), [SCI autonomic dysreflexia](LESSON:spinal-cord-injury-autonomic-dysreflexia), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Multi-patient matrices: **one unstable neuro** client outranks three “busy but stable” tasks.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Airway** and **aspiration** beat paperwork.
- **New weakness + speech** beats mild headache in stable chronic migraine.
- **Post-tPA** sudden headache + **BP** spike → **emergency** evaluation.
- **AD** with pounding headache in SCI → **immediate** nursing sequence per policy.
- **Delegate** only stable, non-judgmental tasks.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** You must choose among four clients: (A) **post-tPA** with **sudden severe headache** and **BP 200/110**, (B) stable **migraine** with **pain 6/10**, (C) **SCI** with **AD** symptoms, (D) routine **med pass**.

**Fork:** **See Client A first**—**intracranial hemorrhage** suspicion after thrombolytic is **life-threatening**; then address **C**, then **B**, then **D** with clear **handoff** communication.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Meningitis and Isolation Precautions** combines **CNS infection** recognition with **isolation** (droplet/contact themes per pathogen and policy), **antibiotic timing** after cultures when safe, and **neuro checks** for **seizures** or **rising ICP**. NCLEX tests **first actions** for **fever + neck stiffness + altered LOC** vignettes.

Connect [meningitis assessment](LESSON:meningitis-isolation-assessment), [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. **Isolation** before wandering the halls and **culture discipline** remain classic teaching points.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:

- **Kernig/Brudzinski** as supporting clues—not standalone diagnosis.
- **Petechial** rash with fever → **emergent** pathways when meningococcemia suggested.
- **Contacts**: public health themes when applicable.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A young adult has **fever**, **neck stiffness**, **photophobia**, and **confusion**. **Isolation** masks are at the door.

**Fork:** **Implement precautions** per policy, **obtain cultures** before antibiotics when feasible and **not crashing**, **monitor** for **seizures** and **LOC**, **notify provider** urgently.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Migraine vs Stroke Red Flags
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Migraine vs Stroke Red Flags | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Migraine vs Stroke Red Flags — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Migraine vs Stroke Red Flags** focuses on **acute neurovascular** care: time-sensitive **reperfusion** eligibility, **bleeding risk** with thrombolytics, **aspiration** prevention, and **neuro checks** after intervention. NCLEX-RN rewards knowing **what delays harm** (silent aspiration, missed deficit, hypotension) versus **routine tasks**.

Differentiate **ischemic** versus **hemorrhagic** patterns when the stem gives **CT** or **presentation** clues—**tPA/TNK** themes apply only when **contraindications** are absent per item framing.

Integrate [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [stroke & ICP nursing gold](LESSON:stroke-increased-icp-gold), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Watch for **tPA exclusion** traps (recent surgery, uncontrolled HTN, improving symptoms) and **hemorrhagic conversion** clues.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **FAST**-style recognition themes; **last known well** documentation.
- **NPO** and **aspiration** precautions when swallow at risk.
- **Thrombolytic**: strict inclusion/exclusion per protocol—**bleeding checks** after.
- **Post-stroke**: DVT prophylaxis per orders, **mobility** when safe, **mood** screening themes.
- **Avoid** giving aspirin before **hemorrhagic** stroke ruled out when stem implies bleed.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 71-year-old arrives with **sudden left arm weakness** and **slurred speech**; **last known well** 70 minutes ago. **BP** is **188/96**, **glucose** **6.2 mmol/L** (Canadian stem), **CT** reportedly shows **no bleed**.

**Fork:** **Activate stroke pathway** themes—**continuous monitoring**, **NPO**, **prepare** for **thrombolytic** eligibility screening per protocol, **avoid** **hypotension** from unnecessary antihypertensives unless ordered for extreme BP—communicate **neuro changes** immediately after intervention.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Dysphagia and Aspiration Precautions
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Dysphagia and Aspiration Precautions | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Dysphagia and Aspiration Precautions — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Dysphagia and Aspiration Precautions** focuses on **acute neurovascular** care: time-sensitive **reperfusion** eligibility, **bleeding risk** with thrombolytics, **aspiration** prevention, and **neuro checks** after intervention. NCLEX-RN rewards knowing **what delays harm** (silent aspiration, missed deficit, hypotension) versus **routine tasks**.

Differentiate **ischemic** versus **hemorrhagic** patterns when the stem gives **CT** or **presentation** clues—**tPA/TNK** themes apply only when **contraindications** are absent per item framing.

Integrate [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [stroke & ICP nursing gold](LESSON:stroke-increased-icp-gold), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Watch for **tPA exclusion** traps (recent surgery, uncontrolled HTN, improving symptoms) and **hemorrhagic conversion** clues.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **FAST**-style recognition themes; **last known well** documentation.
- **NPO** and **aspiration** precautions when swallow at risk.
- **Thrombolytic**: strict inclusion/exclusion per protocol—**bleeding checks** after.
- **Post-stroke**: DVT prophylaxis per orders, **mobility** when safe, **mood** screening themes.
- **Avoid** giving aspirin before **hemorrhagic** stroke ruled out when stem implies bleed.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 71-year-old arrives with **sudden left arm weakness** and **slurred speech**; **last known well** 70 minutes ago. **BP** is **188/96**, **glucose** **6.2 mmol/L** (Canadian stem), **CT** reportedly shows **no bleed**.

**Fork:** **Activate stroke pathway** themes—**continuous monitoring**, **NPO**, **prepare** for **thrombolytic** eligibility screening per protocol, **avoid** **hypotension** from unnecessary antihypertensives unless ordered for extreme BP—communicate **neuro changes** immediately after intervention.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

**Related:** [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window) · [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning) · [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Lumbar Puncture Nursing Care — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Lumbar Puncture Nursing Care** is core NCLEX-RN neurological nursing: you translate **assessment** into **risk**, protect **airway and aspiration**, watch for **sudden focal deficits** or **altered LOC**, and **communicate** with objective data. Boards hide urgency behind “stable” wording—compare **new change** to baseline when the stem allows.

Canadian items may use **metric** vitals and interprofessional notes; **first action** logic matches US stems: **ABC**, **neuro checks**, **seizure safety**, **ICP-friendly positioning** per orders, then **notify** when thresholds are met.

Cross-link [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance

Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Assessment**: LOC, pupils, motor strength by side, speech, facial symmetry, sensation when ordered.
- **Safety**: seizure precautions, bed low, pads, **nothing per mouth** if dysphagia suspected until screened.
- **Escalation**: new deficit, sustained **GCS** drop, **repeated seizures**, **post-tPA** neuro change, **ICP** red flags.
- **Teaching** (when stable): medication timing, injury prevention, when to call EMS.
- **Documentation**: objective trends and times for neuro events.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client with an acute neurological concern related to **Lumbar Puncture Nursing Care** develops **worsening confusion** and **unequal grips** during your shift. **Vitals** show **rising blood pressure** and **irregular respiratory effort**.

**Fork:** Your priority is **focused neuro reassessment**, **airway and oxygenation support** per orders, **seizure precautions** if indicated, and **notify the provider** with **SBAR** including **time of symptom change**—not routine hygiene first.

sections.heading: Takeaways

- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: EEG Nursing Care

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: EEG Nursing Care — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**EEG Nursing Care** covers **seizure recognition**, **airway protection**, **rescue medications** per orders, and **continuous monitoring** for **recurrence** or **status epilepticus**. Nurses prevent **injury**, time events, and prepare for **labs** and **EEG** themes when instability persists.

Medication items probe **therapeutic monitoring** (levels, sedation, rash) and **patient teaching** for adherence and driving safety when stable.

Use [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Status epilepticus stems push **timed benzodiazepine** pathways and **airway** first.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Types**: focal aware/impaired, generalized tonic-clonic, absence themes—match **safety** to type.
- **Status**: treat as **emergency**; protect airway; **timed** rescue meds per ACLS/policy.
- **Meds**: titrate per orders; watch **SJS** rash signals, **hyponatremia** with some agents when stem cues.
- **EEG**: reduce stimuli; safety with **ambulatory** EEG themes when shown.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client has **back-to-back tonic-clonic seizures** without full recovery between. **SpO₂** drifts to **88%** with **secretions**.

**Fork:** **Protect airway**, **call for help**, **give rescue** therapy per order/protocol, **prepare** for **intubation** themes if ventilation fails—**not** leaving to complete another client’s discharge teaching.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: VP Shunt Complications
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: VP Shunt Complications | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: VP Shunt Complications — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**VP Shunt Complications** ties **intracranial pressure dynamics** to **assessment** and **interventions**: **head-of-bed** per order, **avoid harmful neck flexion**, **sedation/analgesia** themes, **osmotherapy** per protocol, and **early recognition** of **herniation** patterns. NCLEX punishes **rough suctioning** or **clustered noxious stimuli** without a plan when ICP risk is high.

Pair [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [stroke & ICP nursing gold](LESSON:stroke-increased-icp-gold), [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Items contrast **early subtle** changes with **late** **Cushing** patterns—choose escalation when late signs appear.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Early**: headache, nausea, subtle pupil change, BP trends.
- **Late**: **Cushing** triad patterns, posturing, rapid **GCS** drop.
- **Positioning**: **head midline**, **HOB** per order—avoid **extreme** hip flexion if contraindicated.
- **Shunt**: **reduced** ICP signs with infection can still be **obstruction**—report **worsening** headache or vomiting.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Post **TBI**, the client’s **GCS** drops from **14 to 10**, **pupil** becomes **sluggish** on one side, and **BP** rises.

**Fork:** **ICP crisis** until cleared—**notify neurosurgery/provider**, **elevate HOB** per order, **avoid** **jugular** compression, **prepare** for **mannitol/hypertonic saline** per orders, **frequent neuro checks**.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Which Neuro Patient Is Unstable?
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Which Neuro Patient Is Unstable? | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Which Neuro Patient Is Unstable? — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Which Neuro Patient Is Unstable?** trains **NGN-style prioritization**: pick the client with **airway compromise**, **new unilateral weakness**, **seizing activity**, **sudden LOC drop**, **Cushing triad**, **autonomic dysreflexia**, or **post-tPA bleeding** over stable teaching or routine meds. Boards reward **objective instability** over polite requests.

Anchor with [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), [SCI autonomic dysreflexia](LESSON:spinal-cord-injury-autonomic-dysreflexia), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Multi-patient matrices: **one unstable neuro** client outranks three “busy but stable” tasks.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Airway** and **aspiration** beat paperwork.
- **New weakness + speech** beats mild headache in stable chronic migraine.
- **Post-tPA** sudden headache + **BP** spike → **emergency** evaluation.
- **AD** with pounding headache in SCI → **immediate** nursing sequence per policy.
- **Delegate** only stable, non-judgmental tasks.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** You must choose among four clients: (A) **post-tPA** with **sudden severe headache** and **BP 200/110**, (B) stable **migraine** with **pain 6/10**, (C) **SCI** with **AD** symptoms, (D) routine **med pass**.

**Fork:** **See Client A first**—**intracranial hemorrhage** suspicion after thrombolytic is **life-threatening**; then address **C**, then **B**, then **D** with clear **handoff** communication.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Cranial Nerves for Nurses
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Cranial Nerves for Nurses | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Cranial Nerves for Nurses — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Cranial Nerves for Nurses** is core NCLEX-RN neurological nursing: you translate **assessment** into **risk**, protect **airway and aspiration**, watch for **sudden focal deficits** or **altered LOC**, and **communicate** with objective data. Boards hide urgency behind “stable” wording—compare **new change** to baseline when the stem allows.

Canadian items may use **metric** vitals and interprofessional notes; **first action** logic matches US stems: **ABC**, **neuro checks**, **seizure safety**, **ICP-friendly positioning** per orders, then **notify** when thresholds are met.

Cross-link [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Assessment**: LOC, pupils, motor strength by side, speech, facial symmetry, sensation when ordered.
- **Safety**: seizure precautions, bed low, pads, **nothing per mouth** if dysphagia suspected until screened.
- **Escalation**: new deficit, sustained **GCS** drop, **repeated seizures**, **post-tPA** neuro change, **ICP** red flags.
- **Teaching** (when stable): medication timing, injury prevention, when to call EMS.
- **Documentation**: objective trends and times for neuro events.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client with an acute neurological concern related to **Cranial Nerves for Nurses** develops **worsening confusion** and **unequal grips** during your shift. **Vitals** show **rising blood pressure** and **irregular respiratory effort**.

**Fork:** Your priority is **focused neuro reassessment**, **airway and oxygenation support** per orders, **seizure precautions** if indicated, and **notify the provider** with **SBAR** including **time of symptom change**—not routine hygiene first.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Stroke: Ischemic vs Hemorrhagic — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Stroke: Ischemic vs Hemorrhagic** focuses on **acute neurovascular** care: time-sensitive **reperfusion** eligibility, **bleeding risk** with thrombolytics, **aspiration** prevention, and **neuro checks** after intervention. NCLEX-RN rewards knowing **what delays harm** (silent aspiration, missed deficit, hypotension) versus **routine tasks**.

Differentiate **ischemic** versus **hemorrhagic** patterns when the stem gives **CT** or **presentation** clues—**tPA/TNK** themes apply only when **contraindications** are absent per item framing.

Integrate [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [stroke & ICP nursing gold](LESSON:stroke-increased-icp-gold), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Watch for **tPA exclusion** traps (recent surgery, uncontrolled HTN, improving symptoms) and **hemorrhagic conversion** clues.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **FAST**-style recognition themes; **last known well** documentation.
- **NPO** and **aspiration** precautions when swallow at risk.
- **Thrombolytic**: strict inclusion/exclusion per protocol—**bleeding checks** after.
- **Post-stroke**: DVT prophylaxis per orders, **mobility** when safe, **mood** screening themes.
- **Avoid** giving aspirin before **hemorrhagic** stroke ruled out when stem implies bleed.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 71-year-old arrives with **sudden left arm weakness** and **slurred speech**; **last known well** 70 minutes ago. **BP** is **188/96**, **glucose** **6.2 mmol/L** (Canadian stem), **CT** reportedly shows **no bleed**.

**Fork:** **Activate stroke pathway** themes—**continuous monitoring**, **NPO**, **prepare** for **thrombolytic** eligibility screening per protocol, **avoid** **hypotension** from unnecessary antihypertensives unless ordered for extreme BP—communicate **neuro changes** immediately after intervention.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:

- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.

Watch for **tPA exclusion** traps (recent surgery, uncontrolled HTN, improving symptoms) and **hemorrhagic conversion** clues.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **FAST**-style recognition themes; **last known well** documentation.
- **NPO** and **aspiration** precautions when swallow at risk.
- **Thrombolytic**: strict inclusion/exclusion per protocol—**bleeding checks** after.
- **Post-stroke**: DVT prophylaxis per orders, **mobility** when safe, **mood** screening themes.
- **Avoid** giving aspirin before **hemorrhagic** stroke ruled out when stem implies bleed.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 71-year-old arrives with **sudden left arm weakness** and **slurred speech**; **last known well** 70 minutes ago. **BP** is **188/96**, **glucose** **6.2 mmol/L** (Canadian stem), **CT** reportedly shows **no bleed**.

**Fork:** **Activate stroke pathway** themes—**continuous monitoring**, **NPO**, **prepare** for **thrombolytic** eligibility screening per protocol, **avoid** **hypotension** from unnecessary antihypertensives unless ordered for extreme BP—communicate **neuro changes** immediately after intervention.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Traumatic Brain Injury
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Traumatic Brain Injury | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Traumatic Brain Injury — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Traumatic Brain Injury** ties **intracranial pressure dynamics** to **assessment** and **interventions**: **head-of-bed** per order, **avoid harmful neck flexion**, **sedation/analgesia** themes, **osmotherapy** per protocol, and **early recognition** of **herniation** patterns. NCLEX punishes **rough suctioning** or **clustered noxious stimuli** without a plan when ICP risk is high.

Pair [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [stroke & ICP nursing gold](LESSON:stroke-increased-icp-gold), [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Items contrast **early subtle** changes with **late** **Cushing** patterns—choose escalation when late signs appear.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Early**: headache, nausea, subtle pupil change, BP trends.
- **Late**: **Cushing** triad patterns, posturing, rapid **GCS** drop.
- **Positioning**: **head midline**, **HOB** per order—avoid **extreme** hip flexion if contraindicated.
- **Shunt**: **reduced** ICP signs with infection can still be **obstruction**—report **worsening** headache or vomiting.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Post **TBI**, the client’s **GCS** drops from **14 to 10**, **pupil** becomes **sluggish** on one side, and **BP** rises.

**Fork:** **ICP crisis** until cleared—**notify neurosurgery/provider**, **elevate HOB** per order, **avoid** **jugular** compression, **prepare** for **mannitol/hypertonic saline** per orders, **frequent neuro checks**.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

**Related:** [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window) · [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning) · [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Concussion Nursing Care — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Concussion Nursing Care** is core NCLEX-RN neurological nursing: you translate **assessment** into **risk**, protect **airway and aspiration**, watch for **sudden focal deficits** or **altered LOC**, and **communicate** with objective data. Boards hide urgency behind “stable” wording—compare **new change** to baseline when the stem allows.

Canadian items may use **metric** vitals and interprofessional notes; **first action** logic matches US stems: **ABC**, **neuro checks**, **seizure safety**, **ICP-friendly positioning** per orders, then **notify** when thresholds are met.

Cross-link [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:

- **Safety**: seizure precautions, bed low, pads, **nothing per mouth** if dysphagia suspected until screened.
- **Escalation**: new deficit, sustained **GCS** drop, **repeated seizures**, **post-tPA** neuro change, **ICP** red flags.
- **Teaching** (when stable): medication timing, injury prevention, when to call EMS.
- **Documentation**: objective trends and times for neuro events.

sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client with an acute neurological concern related to **Concussion Nursing Care** develops **worsening confusion** and **unequal grips** during your shift. **Vitals** show **rising blood pressure** and **irregular respiratory effort**.

**Fork:** Your priority is **focused neuro reassessment**, **airway and oxygenation support** per orders, **seizure precautions** if indicated, and **notify the provider** with **SBAR** including **time of symptom change**—not routine hygiene first.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:

- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Spinal Cord Injury: Levels and Motor/Sensory Loss
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Spinal Cord Injury: Levels and Motor/Sensory Loss | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Spinal Cord Injury: Levels and Motor/Sensory Loss — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Spinal Cord Injury: Levels and Motor/Sensory Loss** applies to **spinal cord injury** clients: **level** determines expected **motor/sensory** loss, **neurogenic shock** (hypotension + relative bradycardia themes), and **autonomic dysreflexia** above the lesion. Nursing priorities include **hemodynamic monitoring**, **bladder/bowel** programs, **skin protection**, and **rapid** response to **sudden hypertension** with **headache**.

Link [SCI autonomic dysreflexia](LESSON:spinal-cord-injury-autonomic-dysreflexia), [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. **AD** questions reward **sitting up**, **removing noxious stimulus**, and **antihypertensive** per order—not ignoring sudden **BP spike** in T6+ injuries.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Levels**: Cervical vs thoracic loss patterns on exams—know **phrenic** risk with high cervical themes.
- **Neurogenic shock**: fluids/vasopressors per orders—different from **spinal shock** wording in some items.
- **AD**: trigger removal, **sit up**, **loosen** constrictive clothing, **notify**—not “finish dressing change first.”
- **Skin and DVT** prevention across rehab phases.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A **T4 SCI** client on the ward reports **pounding headache** and **flushed skin** 20 minutes after **Foley occlusion** is suspected.

**Fork:** **Autonomic dysreflexia**—**sit the patient up**, **loosen clothing**, **check catheter** and **rectum** for noxious stimulus per training, **monitor BP**, **notify provider**—**not** “reassure and continue rounds.”

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario

sections.body:
- **Airway** and **aspiration** beat paperwork.
- **New weakness + speech** beats mild headache in stable chronic migraine.
- **Post-tPA** sudden headache + **BP** spike → **emergency** evaluation.
- **AD** with pounding headache in SCI → **immediate** nursing sequence per policy.
- **Delegate** only stable, non-judgmental tasks.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** You must choose among four clients: (A) **post-tPA** with **sudden severe headache** and **BP 200/110**, (B) stable **migraine** with **pain 6/10**, (C) **SCI** with **AD** symptoms, (D) routine **med pass**.

**Fork:** **See Client A first**—**intracranial hemorrhage** suspicion after thrombolytic is **life-threatening**; then address **C**, then **B**, then **D** with clear **handoff** communication.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

preTest.question: For Delirium vs Dementia vs Depression, which action best matches NCLEX-RN prioritization?
options: Complete discharge paperwork before reporting a sudden drop in GCS
Reassess airway, oxygenation, and neuro status; notify the provider when acute change criteria are met
Send the nursing assistant alone to evaluate new-onset unilateral weakness
Delay assessment to finish a scheduled non-urgent phone call
preTest.rationale: Neurological deterioration requires immediate reassessment, airway and oxygenation protection, and timely provider communication before routine or deferrable tasks.

preTest.question: A client with risk related to Delirium vs Dementia vs Depression reports new numbness and slurred speech. What is the priority?
options: Offer juice and crackers for comfort
Activate stroke precautions, obtain vitals and glucose if ordered, and notify the provider with objective findings
Ambulate the client to rule out anxiety
Wait for the next scheduled neuro check in four hours
preTest.rationale: New focal neuro deficits with speech change are treated as time-sensitive until evaluated; nursing prioritizes assessment, safety, and urgent communication.

postTest.question: Which finding most urgently escalates care in a neuro context for Delirium vs Dementia vs Depression?
options: Mild dry lips in afebrile client
Sudden asymmetric pupils with declining LOC after head injury
Request for a second pillow
Stable chronic tremor unchanged from baseline
postTest.rationale: Anisocoria with declining consciousness suggests herniation or expanding mass until proven otherwise and requires immediate escalation and monitoring.

postTest.question: When is patient teaching about Delirium vs Dementia vs Depression most appropriate?
options: During active seizure or unprotected airway risk
After stabilization when the client is alert, oriented, and able to participate
Only at home without nurse follow-up
Before any assessment to save time
postTest.rationale: Education is effective and safe after life threats are stabilized and the patient can engage—not during active seizure or airway compromise.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Meningitis and Isolation Precautions** combines **CNS infection** recognition with **isolation** (droplet/contact themes per pathogen and policy), **antibiotic timing** after cultures when safe, and **neuro checks** for **seizures** or **rising ICP**. NCLEX tests **first actions** for **fever + neck stiffness + altered LOC** vignettes.

Connect [meningitis assessment](LESSON:meningitis-isolation-assessment), [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. **Isolation** before wandering the halls and **culture discipline** remain classic teaching points.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Migraine vs Stroke Red Flags
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Migraine vs Stroke Red Flags | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN neurological review: Migraine vs Stroke Red Flags — assessment, safety, red flags, aspiration and ICP considerations, Canada- and US-friendly practice framing, and clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Migraine vs Stroke Red Flags** focuses on **acute neurovascular** care: time-sensitive **reperfusion** eligibility, **bleeding risk** with thrombolytics, **aspiration** prevention, and **neuro checks** after intervention. NCLEX-RN rewards knowing **what delays harm** (silent aspiration, missed deficit, hypotension) versus **routine tasks**.

Differentiate **ischemic** versus **hemorrhagic** patterns when the stem gives **CT** or **presentation** clues—**tPA/TNK** themes apply only when **contraindications** are absent per item framing.

Integrate [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window), [stroke & ICP nursing gold](LESSON:stroke-increased-icp-gold), [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay assessment**, **delegate unstable neuro checks** to UAP, or **teach** before **stabilizing** hypoxia, airway risk, or acute ICP signs. Expect **SBAR** and **time documentation** around stroke and seizure events. Watch for **tPA exclusion** traps (recent surgery, uncontrolled HTN, improving symptoms) and **hemorrhagic conversion** clues.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **FAST**-style recognition themes; **last known well** documentation.
- **NPO** and **aspiration** precautions when swallow at risk.
- **Thrombolytic**: strict inclusion/exclusion per protocol—**bleeding checks** after.
- **Post-stroke**: DVT prophylaxis per orders, **mobility** when safe, **mood** screening themes.
- **Avoid** giving aspirin before **hemorrhagic** stroke ruled out when stem implies bleed.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 71-year-old arrives with **sudden left arm weakness** and **slurred speech**; **last known well** 70 minutes ago. **BP** is **188/96**, **glucose** **6.2 mmol/L** (Canadian stem), **CT** reportedly shows **no bleed**.

**Fork:** **Activate stroke pathway** themes—**continuous monitoring**, **NPO**, **prepare** for **thrombolytic** eligibility screening per protocol, **avoid** **hypotension** from unnecessary antihypertensives unless ordered for extreme BP—communicate **neuro changes** immediately after intervention.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **LOC + pupils + motor + speech** are your rapid screen.
- **Aspiration** risk follows **dysphagia** and **altered LOC**—**NPO** until cleared when ordered.
- **Synthesis:** unstable neuro = **assess + protect + notify** before teaching.

**Related:** [stroke assessment & tPA window](LESSON:stroke-assessment-tpa-window) · [increased ICP positioning](LESSON:increased-icp-positioning) · [seizure precautions](LESSON:seizure-precautions-rescue-meds) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).
